The formation type "Integration with SAP Build" refers to a configuration within SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) that enables the integration of SAP systems with the SAP Build suite, specifically for scenarios like process automation and application development. This formation type is used when you want to connect your SAP system (e.g., SAP Success Factors) to services like SAP Build Process Automation or other SAP Build solutions. 

    • Navigate to Global Account → System Landscape → Formations -> Customer View -> Click Create Formation

    • Give a formation name and Select: Integration with SAP Build

    • Add the following systems in the Include Systems step of the wizard (IMPORTANT NOTE: All of them should be in the same BTP Subaccount):
      • Your registered SAP SuccessFactors system
      • Your SAP Build tenant
    • Review the selection

    •  Click Create and check that the formation is created successfully

Step 6: Validate in Event Hub App

As SAP Build is a consumer of events, you need manage event subscriptions in the SAP Cloud Application Event Hub application. Integration dependencies defined in the System Landscape become available as event subscriptions.

Pre-requisite : Assign relevant Event Broker Roles to your user, if roles were just assigned, re-login.

    • Open the Event Hub Application

 Click on “All Available Subscriptions” and select the subscription name that we have created in the above step.

 Enable the SFSF event subscription.

Click on Enable.

Check that the Subscription status is “Active”.

Step 7: Create and publish an events project

Now you need to create an event project from the SAP Build Lobby which will be published to Library so that we can use it as an event trigger in the SBPA process. Follow the steps mentioned below:

  • Create an event project. Go to the SAP BTP Sub Account -> Lobby -> Connectors ->Events ->Create.

  • As the Success Factors system and BTP Subaccount are now connected and we will be able to choose events based on the connected systems, choose Unified Customer Landscape.

Note: Read Discovering Business Events from Lobby for SAP Build Process Automation : Manage Credit Memo Request for more ways to create Event Projects from SAP Build Lobby.

Select SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding Events.

Select Onboarding Process Step.

Review all the events and payloads and click on Add.

Create the event project. Release and publish it to the library. Note : Publishing is mandatory to be able to use the events as event trigger in any SBPA Process.

After the event project is created successfully, its listed in the Lobby.

Step 8: Create and Deploy a Sample process in SAP Build Process Automation

    • Go to SAP Build Process Automation from the BTP Subaccount
    • Create a new Process Project,  the empty process would look like the following.

    • Click on Add a trigger ->  “Wait for an Event” from the trigger options to add an event trigger to the project.

  • Search and Add the required event from the library.

After creation of event trigger check that the event trigger is added and the Outputs tab shows the event payload.  This payload can be further used in the business process for further processing like approvals, decisions, subprocesses , actions etc.

    • Add a simple action (e.g., approval or email) or any set of actions required for your business process. A sample process could look like the following.

    • Release and Deploy the process by selecting appropriate environment.
    • Test by triggering the event from Success Factors.

The below step confirms that the Personal Data Collection is completed and this event should now trigger our deployed SBPA Process.

 Go to SAP BTP Subaccount -> Lobby -> Monitoring -> Process and Workflow Instances and check that the process is in Running status.

 You can open the process instance to see further details. For example, in the below logs the task is waiting to be approved in My Inbox.

 Click My Inbox to review and approve the task.

 Note that the simple approval process based on SuccessFactors event is completed.

 Below is the sample Logs view for the completed process instance.

Troubleshooting Tips

    • If systems aren’t showing in formations: double-check region/subaccount match
    • Event Hub app not showing dependencies? Check your user role + re-login
    • Not receiving events? Confirm SuccessFactors is emitting the specific event (some are tenant-configurable). Also check the section Lobby -> Monitoring -> Acquired Events -> Business Events to confirm that business events are received in SBPA.

Conclusion

You’ve now connected SAP SuccessFactors to SAP Build via SAP Cloud Application Event Hub! This architecture lays the groundwork for robust, event-driven automations—like onboarding workflows—all without writing complex integration code or going through complicated setup process.

References

Please refer to the below blogs for event driven architecture in SAP Build Process Automation:

Business Event Triggers in SAP Build Process Automation for SAP S/4HANA Cloud : The Customer Return (BKP) Use Case

Business Event Triggers in SAP Build Process Automation for SAP S/4HANA: Manage Billing Block on Sales Orders

Business Event Triggers in SAP Build Process Automation for SAP S/4HANA: Manage Credit Block on Sales Orders

Discovering Business Events from Lobby for SAP Build Process Automation : Manage Credit Memo Request

Event Mesh and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup for SAP Build Process Automation
Incorporating Custom Events from Lobby into SAP Build Process Automation

Thanks to my co-author @emanuil_ivanov  for his valuable support and collaboration for the content of this blog !

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite: What’s New in Q3/2025
entry.content:
Another quarter has come to an end and it’s time for us to review the most recent updates and improvements delivered to SAP Integration Suite. Whether you’re an integration architect, developer, or business leader, these new features are designed to help you get the most out of your hybrid landscape. Read on to discover what’s new and how these enhancements can support your organisation’s integration goals.

Message status overview monitoring for large tenants

One awaited featured has been released to the Cloud Integration capability of SAP Integration Suite to improve Message Status Overview monitoring and support larger time intervals through data preaggregation. Now the monitoring option can be utilized for extended periods on tenants with a high volume of message processing logs.

Artificial Intelligence

AI-based integration flow generation with mediation steps

Earlier this year, the generation of integration flows with the assistance of AI was made available to customers. This enable users to generate an integration flow with sender and receiver systems based on a scenario description provided in natural language. You can read more about it here.

In Q3/2025 we have enhanced this feature to include additional mediation steps. This means that now, users can provide even more detailed scenarios and the tool identifies the details and generates a comprehensive integration flow with the described components, systems, and actions. Get a first glimpse into how to use this feature in the blog post: SAP Integration Suite – Generative AI based Integration Flow Generation with Mediation Steps.

 Business Accelerators

Joule copilot-based conversational search and discovery of content

Joule has arrived at the SAP Business Accelerator Hub in Q3 to help users find the right content throughconversational context-based search. The Joule-based discovery provides user guidance to search different artifacts and access cohesive information for other relevant sources, such as help documentation.

Note: The feature is currently in the beta phase and you can find more details on availability here.  

Connectivity and Adapters

The latest releases of SAP Integration Suite introduced a new array of adapters and connectors designed to streamline and enhance connectivity, improve data flow, and foster a more integrated digital landscape for businesses leveraging SAP solutions.

AI adapter

The recently released AI adapter for SAP Integration Suite functions as a receiver adapter and accelerates connectivity to the Generative AI Hub (SAP AI Core) and other AI providers, as needed by customer (BYOM).

 The benefit of this adapter is that it increases connectivity options to integrate with both SAP and third-party systems and applications. How it works: after configuration, data exchange is performed at runtime where the SAP Integration Suite tenant sends the operation request to GenAIHub. The AI Adapter works on the request and sends the data back to the SAP Integration Suite tenant.

Find the AI Adapter at SAP Business Accelerator Hub, read the help documentation, and check out the short demo below:

Further adapters released in Q3 include:

IBM MQ adapter using JMS protocol: client certificate supportSupports Client Certificate based authentication for JMS Variants. A new version 1.3.0 of the IBM MQ adapter supports Client Certificate based authentication for JMS Variants.- Configure the IBM MQ JMS Receiver Adapter
IBM MQ adapter using JMS protocol: client certificate supportSupports On-Premise Connectivity via Cloud Connector for JMS Variants. A new version 1.4.0 of the IBM MQ adapter supports On-Premise Connectivity via Cloud Connector for JMS Variants.

- Configure the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter

- Configure the IBM MQ JMS Receiver Adapter
BigCommerce adapterFacilitates the connectivity to BigCommerce GraphQL & REST Admin APIs, accelerates the implementation time and reduces complexity of connecting to BigCommerce. This adapter is available on SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

- BigCommerce Adapter at SAP Business Accelerator Hub

- BigCommerce Receiver Adapter

🚀🚀Discover the latest content packages, including APIs and Integrations, released on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub by our partners in Q3 2025. These releases come with high-level details to help you understand their features and benefits.

API-Centric & Event-Driven Integrations

Support for reusable API artifacts in Edge Integration Cell

Customers can now leverage components, endpoints, and services across multiple applications, projects, or contexts, without having to perform significant adaptations. The reusability of API artifacts enables companies to implement a centralized security and traffic management system for exposed APIs. It not only enhances compliance and reduces misconfiguration risks but also significantly lowers both the total cost of development and ownership by eliminating redundant configurations and promoting the reuse of existing API artifacts, which means additional API calls are avoided, leading to cost savings. Learn more.

 

Watch the demo from this year's Devtoberfest - Session "Next generation hybrid integration with Edge Integration Cell":

Integration of Advanced Event Mesh with SAP Cloud Application Event Hub

The latest updates allow you to connect your SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh system to SAP Cloud Application Event Hub for the consumption and exposure of events.

SAP Cloud Application Event Hub creates a clear, user-friendly, and adaptable way to easily exchange business events across SAP's cloud landscape. The solution is a key part of SAP's event-driven architecture. With the integration, you can access events from various SAP business systems, published via the hub, directly on SAP Integration Suite's advanced event mesh.

Migration & Modernization

ABAP proxy generation from service interfaces for current users of SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Integration Suite now supports the implementation of existing use cases for the Enterprise Services Repository using an efficient API-first development methodology. This assists users in importing the service interface artifacts from their PI/PO systems to the Cloud Integration workspace. The feature includes the ability to generate and update ABAP Proxies (non-language-specific interface descriptions in Web Services Description Language converted into executable interfaces) in SAP backend or S/4 HANA systems by connecting them to the SAP Integration Suite tenant and it’s available in the multi-cloud environment. Find more details and the prerequisites here.

Migration assessment: Clean core modernization recommendations for BAPIs

This extension of the Migration Assessment capability in SAP Integration Suite makes it possible for users to analyse used BAPIs in SAP Process Orchestration. Additionally, they receive recommendations available in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub to transition from legacy content and adopt a clean core approach, e.g. replacing RFCs (BAPIs) with SOAP/OData APIs, IDOCs with Business Events, Alert Rules by SAP Cloud ALM, and more. Explore all modernization recommendations covered in each area.

Dive Deeper

New learning offerings available

Accelerating Hybrid Integrations with SAP Integration Suite on SUSE Rancher Set up, deploy, and operate Edge Integration Cell on SUSE Rancher Kubernetes EngineTime investment 3h, Record of achievement
Accelerating Hybrid Integrations with SAP Integration Suite on RedHat OpenShift Set up, deploy, and run an Edge Integration Cell on Red Hat OpenShiftTime investment 3h, Record of achievement
Exploring Enterprise Automation with SAPUnderstand how SAP Signavio, SAP Build, and SAP Integration Suite work together to improve efficiency and responsiveness Time investment 1.5h, Record of achievement

What’s next?

  • Integration @ Devtoberfest: watch all sessions
  • Join us at SAP TechEd 2025: explore the Integration track and register

entry.title: Clean Core by Design: Event Enablement Cockpit as a Core Integration Enabler in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Enterprise Event Enablement is one of the core components of the modern SAP Enterprise Integration Architecture for SAP S/4 HANA, which enables seamless exchange of business events across different platforms for Event-Driven communication. Events from SAP S/4 can be sent to SAP BTP Integration Suite Event Mesh/ AEM/ SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, where they can be consumed by any recipient based on the requirement.
To exchange events between SAP S/4 and  BTP EM, a communication connection setup is required. This connection is maintained by the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, which enables the data flow from the SAP S/4 HANA to the Business Event Consumer, and BTP services play it's role as the message broker(Integration Suite EM/ AEM).

A channel represents a single connection to a service instance of the SAP BTP, Event Mesh. For more information, see Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels.

Note: Channel configurations can be done via SAP Event Mesh, SAP Advance Event Mesh or SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, depending on your scenario and architecture, in this blog we will be going through SAP EM. For SAP ECC, you need ASAPIO for Events Configuration/ Enablement because Standard Business Events, like they are natively available in S/4 are not available in ECC.



SAP S/4 Event Producers

Non SAP S/4 Event Producers

Create a Destination through the SM59 transaction and setup the OAuth 2.0 configuration on the S/4 HANA system, both must be configured as per the service key instance of the corresponding event mesh instance.
After the channel creation is completed, you can list which events can be part of this channel, and an outbound binding needs to be setup to publish the events of an S/4 HANA system.
All administration, configuration, and monitoring steps are available in transaction SPRO under the Enterprise Event Enablement Node,

Under the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, below are the authorization objects and roles that are available,
Under the Hood of Enterprise Event Enablement Framework, the available authorization objects and roles are below,

Authorization Objects: /IWXBE/ADM, /IWXBE/USR, /IWXBE/SUP

Roles: SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_ADM, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_BUSI, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_MDT

Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels

  1. Create an EM Service Instance
  2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
    1. Creating and Managing Channels
      1. Create an RFC Destination
      2. OAuth 2.0 Client Config
      3. Manage Channel and Parameters

1. Create an EM Service Instance
Create a service instance for the EM,

  1. Go to your SAP BTP cockpit global account and subaccount.
      - To see how the navigation works in the SAP BTP cockpit, refer to Navigate in the Cockpit.
  2. Expand Services  Instances and Subscriptions.
  3. Choose Create.
  4. Choose Event Mesh and the default service plan.


  5. Choose your Cloud Foundry space, enter an instance name, and choose Next.

  6. Specify parameters using a JSON file for the Service Key.
    Example:
{
  "emname": "<emname>",
  "namespace": "<namespace e.g. a/b/c>",
  "version": "1.1.0",
  "options": {
    "management": true,
    "messagingrest": true,
    "messaging": true
  },
  "rules": {
    "queueRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    },
    "topicRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    }
  },
	"xs-security": { //optional//
     "oauth2-configuration": { 
      "credential-types": [
        "binding-secret",
        "x509"
      ]
    }
  }
}

2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
Once the service instance key is created, it will have all the necessary information to make a connection, it will be used in the automatic process creation and configuration of the destination and OAuth 2.0 config, which will be handled by the Event Enablement Framework itself.

To create a channel using a service key, proceed as follows:
1. Execute the Event Enablement Configuration.
2. Press the via Service Key (Create new channel via Service Key) button.


3. Enter the following details:
    - a. Channel
    - b. Description
    - c. Destination (Enter your preferred RFC destination name)
    - d. OAuth 2.0 Configuration (Enter your preferred name. The name must not have been used yet)
4. Enter the service key.

5. Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

2.i. Creating and Managing Channels

2.i.a. Creating RFC destination,
Steps:
1. Go to BTP -> Event Mesh -> Cloud Foundry Environment, to get the certificates, follow the steps below:
     - Launch the token endpoint URL, https://<enterprise messaging url>/oauth/token that you receive from the service instance of SAP EM.
     - Export all certificates from the certificates path.
2. Logon to the SAP S/4HANA, on-premise system that has the SAP_GWFND component.
3. Open the transaction STRUST, select the required node SSL Client (Standard) and import all the certificates.
4. Open the transaction SMICM, select Administration ICM Exit Soft Local to clear the cache.
5. In SPRO, open SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
6. Open Manage RFC Destination.
7. Create the RFC destination, give name and description and select the connection type as G and Save.
8. Under Technical Settings, in Target System Setting, in the Host field, enter the domain name of the <app-url> given in the URI, example, wss://<app-url>/protocols/mqtt311ws of the service instance key in the mqtt311ws protocol.
9. (Optional) Under Technical Settings, in HTTP Proxy Settings, enter the proxy details.
10. Under the Logon and Security tab, in the Security option, enable SSL as Active and import the SSL Certificate from the certificate list and save.

2.i.b. OAuth 2.0 Client Config

Steps:

1. Inside SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
2. Open the Manage OAuth Client Setup and Click on Create.
3. In OAuth 2.0 Client Profile field, select the /IWXBE/MGW_MQTT profile and Enter a configuration name, and in Client ID, enter the EM service instance Client ID and press ok.
4. Under the Details section, choose the Administration and place the Client Secret you receive from the EM service instance.
5. Under Authorization Server Settings, enter the following:
   - In the Authorization Endpoint field, enter the token endpoint URL of the service instance and replace <token> with <authorize>.
   - For the Token Endpoint field, set the token endpoint URL of the service instance.
6. In the Access Settings, under the Resource Access Authentication section, choose Header Field.
7. Select the Grant Type as Client Credentials and enter the proxy settings(if applicable) and save.

 2.i.c. Manage Channel and Parameters

Steps:

1. Go to SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide  SAP NetWeaver  Enterprise Event Enablement  Administration  Channel Connection Settings  Manage Channels and Parameters or execute transaction.
2. Either create the new or select the already created one(based on the requirement).
3. Create/ Configure the output binding configuration,


4. In the Topic Name field, enter the namespace derived from the service key of the given instance(Topic space is the identifier for events that originate from the source)

5. Make the following entries in the Parameters section (mandatory).
- OAuth 2.0 Client Profile: /IWXBE/MGW_AMQP.
- OAuth 2.0 Configuration: Select the configuration name created in OAuth 2.0 Account Client Configuration.
6. Entries can be made in the optional parameters, below are the available parameters:
- Max Reconnect Attempts: Enterprise Event Enablement Framework executes to a defined number of times reestablish the connection in case of connection failure, the value of 0 or no value, the framework tries to connect until the connection is established. The channel is deactivated after reaching the reconnect attempts and still the connection is not established.
- Quality of Service (QoS): There are two values:
   - At Most Once(The message arrives at SAP BTP, EM mostly once)
   - At Least Once(QoS=1, default value if no value is entered).
     This quality of service ensures that the message arrives at SAP BTP, EM at least once)
- Reconnect Waittime:
- The Enterprise Event Enablement framework waits for the specified time(in seconds) and after that tries to reconnect. The framework is setup in such a way when the failure occurs, it increases the waittime till it reaches the Max Reconnect waittime(1800 seconds). The default reconnect waittime is 10 seconds, if the Max Reconnect Attempts value is not maintained, the framework tries to reconnect until the connection is established but the channel will not be deactivated during this period.
7. Choose the Save configuration button. or press Enter and select either Active<->Deactivate to activate or deactivate the channel.
- If the channel is active, only then will the events be sent. The channel might be in inactive status if the connection was terminated and reconnects failed.
- The channel will be deactivated during runtime, if the number of reconnect attempts are completed and the connection is still not established.
8. Press Check connection to perform a connection test.
9. If the connection test fails for an active channel, the corresponding error message is displayed. The same applies to an inactive channel.
 
 
Event Discovery Service Activation Step:
Once the Above steps are done, activate the Event Discovery Service and start Maintaining the Event Topics Accordingly,
Procedure

1. In transaction SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to Enterprise Event Enablement Administration.
2. Execute Activate Event Discovery Service and Choose Add Service, add alias as LOCAL and give External Service Name as BROWSER_SRV and then click on Get Services.
3. Select the service and Choose Add Selected Services, save it as local object and choose continue.
4. If the system alias is missing, go to System Aliases pane, choose Add System Alias and select new entries.
5. Enter the Service Doc Identifier as ZBROWSER_SRV_0001 and enter LOCAL in the SAP System Alias field.
6. Enable the Default System checkbox and click on save.
7. In the ICF Nodes pane, choose ICF Node and choose Activate.

 

 Configure the EM Client:

1. Message Clients
Goto EM Application and check the Message Clients( if case there is no, then create one otherwise you use the existing one)


2. Create Queue


3. Queue Subscription

4. Subscribe to the specific topics as per the requirement

5. Create a Webhook(Optional)
Use a webhook only when the consumer application does not support AMQP or MQTT protocols.

Conclusion



Now the setup is completed, try to test it through the test tab, and you are ready to enable the business events from the S/4 system. In this way, you will be able to set up the Event-Driven Architecture effectively within the S/4 system. Through this approach, you can set up a standardized and scalable way of exposing the Business Events in real time, enabling the flow of information through multiple ecosystems across the enterprise landscapes. By applying this approach, you can remove the reliance on the tightly coupled design and be more flexible through a loosely coupled design, which makes all the systems notified instantly.



References:
https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-cloud-application-event-hub/sap-cloud-application-event-hub-service-guide/integration-use-cases
https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-integration-connectivity/creating-and-managing-channels?version=s4_hana
https://help.sap.com/docs/BTP/65de2977205c403bbc107264b8eccf4b/0874895f1f78459f9517da55a11ffebd.html
https://asapio.com/docs/connectors/sap-aem/
https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1

entry.title: From Batch to Event-Driven: Liberating SFTP Data with Micro-Integrations
entry.content:
Introduction

At the end of last year, Micro-Integrations were introduced as a new capability within SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh.

This capability provides lightweight integration functionality to easily connect SaaS and legacy applications, LLMs and AI Agents, messaging services, data stores, etc. directly to your event brokers. It also supports basic transformations, comparable to message mappings.

The keyword here is lightweight. Micro-Integrations are not a replacement for Cloud Integration, it complements it. Cloud Integration is designed to handle integration complexity way beyond the level that the Micro-Integrations capability can handle. Micro-Integrations focus on simplicity, speed, and high throughput use cases.

In practice, using Cloud Integration for simple pass-through scenarios from or to Advanced Event Mesh with no transformation (apart from perhaps a simple message mapping) can be inefficient. In high-volume scenarios (e.g. Event Streaming) it means you’re consuming a lot of messages without leveraging the true strength of Cloud Integration.
This is exactly where the Micro-Integrations capability adds value: handling lightweight, high-throughput integrations, closer to the event broker, while leaving the more complex transformations to Cloud Integration.

In this blog, we will explore the SFTP Source Micro-Integration. It enables you to convert files into JSON messages, publish them to the event mesh, and make them available to your applications at scale in near real-time. From there, the data can be distributed across consuming systems in real time, eliminating the need for complex, polling-based point-to-point integrations.

This blog is structured into five main sections:

  • Introduction
  • The prerequisites
  • The scenario
  • The process
  • Create a 1K broker service in SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh
  • Create a temporary SFTP Server
  • Create the SFTP Source Micro-Integration
  • Configure the flow
  • Build the content-based topic
  • Testing the Micro-Integration
  • Troubleshooting
  • Final thoughts

 

The prerequisites

What you need to run through this scenario from A-Z

  1. Access to SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh, including the required roles:
    1. Mission Control Manager
    2. Micro-Integrations Manager
  1. Sufficient entitlements to create a 1K broker service in the SAP BTP subaccount where Advanced Event Mesh is hosted.
    To verify this:
    1. Navigate to your SAP BTP Subaccount
    2. Open “Entitlements” from the left-hand menu
    3. Scroll down to the service “integration-suite-advanced-event-mesh”
    4. You should see an available quota under the plan “broker-1K”.

  2. An SFTP client installed on your local workstation.
    In the blog I’m using FileZilla Client, but any equivalent tool should do the job.

The scenario

In this example, a retail chain with stores across EMEA has each point-of-sale (POS) system dropping a CSV file containing sales line items onto an SFTP server, folder:
/upload/retail/sales/inbound.
Every night, a batch job processes these files and loads the data into a data warehouse.

In this process, nothing is broken, but this data is valuable and the way it moves holds it hostage.

It’s not traveling in real-time, so inventory systems can’t react to a sell-out until tomorrow, the store dashboard only reflect yesterday’s data, fraud checks run hours too late, etc.

At the same time, making this data available to additional systems, such as an ERP or loyalty application, requires building new, tightly coupled point-to-point integrations with the SFTP server, increasing complexity and maintenance effort, limiting scalability.

Event-Driven Architectures addresses both challenges, it liberates your data. This doesn’t mean replacing the existing components, it means making the data that is already in the organization available in real-time to whoever (system and applications) needs it by putting a broker between the data source (publisher, SFTP server) and the consuming applications (subscribers).

Whenever a CSV file is updated or a new one is dropped, each line item is converted into a JSON message and published as an event, ready for the subscribers to consume it at their pace.

This approach:

✅  Enables real-time data availability
✅  Decouples producers and consumers
✅  Simplifies and accelerates scaling and onboarding/offboarding of applications
✅  Increases reliability through built-in queueing mechanisms in Advanced Event Mesh

Topic Design

For this scenario, we’ll use for the following topic structure:

retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/{region}/{storeId}/{productCategory}/{transactionId}


Advanced Event Mesh supports dynamically subscriptions using wildcards :

  • *   replaces any string of characters
  • >  matches everything beyond it in the hierarchy.

For example :

  • All recorded sales line items (interesting for ERP, data warehouse and loyalty systems) :
    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/>
  • Single store dashboard (for store-042):
    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/emea/store-042/>
  • Product category filtering (e.g. beverages for replenishment):
    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/*/*/beverages/>

The diagram below highlights how everything is connected :

The process

So, let's get started! 🚀

Create a 1K broker service in SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh

Note: if you have already created a broker service, you can skip to step 2 in this section.

  1. Creating the broker
    1. When you open SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh, you open what we refer to as the “Cloud Console” (you can always go back to the cloud console by clicking on the SAP logo on the top left).
    2. Navigate to “Mission Control” -> “Cluster Manager”.
       
    3. There you see an overview of your existing brokers, click “Create Service” on the right top.

    4. Provide at least the following 
      • Service Name
      • Cloud provider and region
      • Service Type: Enterprise
      • Service Class: Broker 1K

    5. Click "Create Service"

  2. Once the service is ready:

    1. Navigate to "Try Me!"

    2. Copy the Client Username and Client Password
      ⚠️Important: you will need this later, so you may want to consider pasting this in a notepad.

       

    3.  Click on “Open Broker Manager”, this opens a page where you can publish messages on topic and queues in your broker and subscribe to these messages as well.
    4. Enter your client password in the connect tab and hit connect (on the left side and right side), now you can try out your broker.⚠️ Remember: keep your client username and password at hand!

Create a temporary SFTP Server

  1. Go to sftpcloud.io 
  2. Click on "Create test SFTP Server".
    This creates a test SFTP Server and it is available for one hour. This should be enough for the duration of this tutorial.
  3. After the creation, you see an overview of:
    • Host
    • Port
    • Username
    • Password
      ⚠️Keep this tab open or copy these credentials to a notepad, we will need them very soon.

Create the SFTP Source Micro-Integration

  1. In the Cloud Console, navigate to "Micro-Integrations"

     

  2.  If this is your first time creating a Micro-Integration, you should click on “Get Started with Micro-Integrations”, in the other case just click “Create Micro-Integration” on the right top.

  3. You are now shown a list of different micro-integrations, let’s find the SFTP Source Micro-Integration. Search for “SFTP” using the search bar on the top left and select the “SFTP Source”.

  4. Now, give the Micro-Integration a name and description, for example: 
    1. Name: sftp-retail-source
    2. Description: Liberates batch sales files from SFTP into real-time event stream
      Click "Next".

  5. Enter a name for the checkpoint queue (e.g. ckpt-sftp-retail) and check the box for “Auto Create Checkpoint queue”. The checkpoint queue will keep track of which files and rows have been published, so the process resumes exactly where it left off after a restart or failure.
    Click “Next”
     
  6. Select your broker.

  7. Scroll down and enter the client username and client password of your broker (copied from step 2 in create the broker section).
    Note: for the purpose of this tutorial we’ll leave the rest as-is. For a real use case, you may want to check the authentication settings.
    Click “Next”.

     

  8. At the summary, click on “Create”.

     

Configure the flow

  1. After the creation of the Micro-Integration, you see a pop-up, in this pop-up click on “Create Flow”, this is where the magic happens.

     

  2. Give your flow a name and description, for example:
    Name: sales-csv-to-lineitem-events
    Description:
    Reads each new or updated sales CSV from the SFTP inbound folder, splits it into individual rows, maps every row to a JSON event, and publishes it to retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/{region}/{storeId}/{productCategory}/{transactionId}

  3. Scroll down to “Source Endpoint” and add your SFTP Server URI in the format:

    sftp://<host>:<port>/upload/retail/sales/inbound

  4. Enter the username and password of the SFTP server created in step 2
  5. Scroll down again, and use the following delimiter file configuration:
    • Event delimiter:  \n
    • Field delimiter: ,
      Note: This means that for each line in the CSV file an event will be created, and that fields are separated with a “,”.

  6. Scroll to Target Endpoint and enter the following Topic Name:
    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1
    For our use case, we will work with content-based topics, but since the topic field is mandatory the topic name we add serves as a placeholder.
  7. Click "Next: Error Handling"

  8.  Optionally, you can enable retries and a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) here. Click “Next: Mappings”.

  9. We can now create a mapping, you’ll see a title that says, “No Source Payload”, under this title click on “Import File” and import the Sample JSON.
    After, use the same file for the target payload. We are not going to build a complex mapping in this example, we will focus more on the content-based topics.
    ⚠️Note: Even though we upload CSV files later, the micro-integration automatically converts them to a JSON structure.

  10. After the files are uploaded, link the source and the target fields, they are all the same, make sure you don’t miss one.
     
  11. If you didn’t miss one, you should see a message saying that all target fields are mapped.

Build the content-based topic

  1. Once the mapping is complete, we construct the topic using constants and payload fields. This is how the topic will look like:

    retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/{region}/{storeId}/{productCategory}/{transactionId}

  2. Hoover to the left side of your screen, and click on the “+” button next to “Constants”, under “SFTP Source” and add the following constants:
    • First levels of the topic taxonomy:
      Data Type: String
      Constant Value: retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1

    • Separator:
      Data Type: String
      Constant Value: retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1

  3. Drag a line from one of the values that we just created to “Smart Topic Destination” and click on “Add transformation”.

  4. Use the Concatenate function and build a transformation that looks this (use “Add input” to add fields):
    • retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1
    • /
    • region
    • /
    • storeId
    • /
    • productCategory
    • /
    • transactionId
      ⚠️Ensure there are no double slashes (//) or the topic does not end with a slash (/)
  5. Click "Apply", then click "Next: Summary"
  6. Click "Create".
  7. After a few seconds, a page is shown where you see your Micro-Integration and the flow you just created listed, if you made it this far, click on “Deploy”. A pop-up will show to confirm, confirm by clicking “Deploy” once more.

  8. You should now see that the flow is running successfully.

Testing the Micro-Integration

  1. After you have created the micro-integration, go to the cloud console, go to “Cluster Manager” and open your broker.
  2. Go to the “Try Me!” tab, copy the client password and click on “Open Broker Manager”.

  3. Enter the client password on the left side of the screen and hit “Connect” on both the “Publisher” and “Subscriber” side.
     
  4. Subscribe to topics such as:
    • retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/>
    • retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/emea/store-042/>
    • retail/sales/lineitem/recorded/v1/*/*/beverages/>
  5. Now, go to FileZilla Client (or another File Transfer Client) and connect to your SFTP Server.

  6. You should see the following folder structure: /upload/retail/sales/inbound
    This was created by the Micro-Integration flow we created throughout step 3.
    Note: During the setup of the MI, we specified the folder structure. The moment we deployed the flow, it connected to the SFTP Server and looked for the folders. It didn’t find these folders so it created it.
  7. Click through until you are in the folder “inbound”.
  8. Download the sample CSV.
  9. Upload the CSV into the folder “inbound”.

  10. After the upload, go back to the “Try Me!” tab and, you should see the messages flowing in. Feel free to play around with the file and subscriptions.

Troubleshooting

  1. If issues occur:
    1. Check if the SFTP server is still active (expires after 1 hour)
    2. Verify credentials (broker vs SFTP)
    3. Ensure no // in topic construction
    4. Confirm client permissions on the broker (when you didn’t create the broker from scratch)
  2. You can view logs via: 
    Micro-Integrations -> Select the SFTP Source MI we just created -> 3 dots on top right -> View Error Logs

     

Final thoughts

If everything works like expected, congratulations, you just developed an SFTP source micro-integration!

From here, you can create queues subscribing to the topics used in this example and have various applications consuming from these queues.

Hope you enjoyed this blog!
Curious to hear your thoughts; did you like this blog, are there any other topics you would like me to cover,… feel free to share in the comments!

Event-driven architecture SAP Integration Suite

entry.title: How to Set Up SAP Cloud Application Event Hub to Consume APM eIoT Rule-Based Events
entry.content:
How to Set Up SAP Cloud Application Event Hub to Consume APM eIoT Rule-Based Events

 

About the Authors

This blog post has been created in cooperation with Lara-Aida Jopp (@LJopp) a business process consultant for SAP EAM, IAM - mainly APM.

Authors:

  • Michel Seifner
  • Lara-Aida Jopp

In case of questions, feel free to leave a comment or to reach out to us!

 

 

Introduction

SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) provides powerful condition-based monitoring of asset health through Rules based on embedded IoT. These rules continuously evaluate sensor data and operational parameters against defined conditions. When a condition is met, the rule can generate an event as its output result.

With SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, these events can be routed to your own applications — enabling you to build custom handlers and business logic that react to asset health changes in real time. Whether you want to trigger maintenance workflows, send notifications, or feed data into analytics pipelines, Event Hub provides the infrastructure to consume these events reliably.

In this blog post, you will learn step by step how to set up SAP Cloud Application Event Hub in your BTP landscape to receive events from APM Rules based on embedded IoT. By the end, you will have a fully configured event routing pipeline from APM through Event Hub to a consumer of your choice — either SAP Integration Suite (recommended) or your own BTP application.

What You Will Achieve

  • A configured Event Hub application connected to your APM system
  • A subscription to the three APM Rules based on embedded IoT event types
  • A working event flow from APM through Event Hub to your consumer — SAP Integration Suite or a custom BTP application

APM Rules based on embedded IoT Event Types

APM publishes three event types through Event Hub:

Event Type Description

sap.apm.MonitoringRule.Run.v1Triggered when a monitoring rule condition is met
sap.apm.DerivationRule.Run.v1Triggered when a derivation rule produces a result
sap.apm.AggregationRule.Run.v1Triggered when an aggregation rule produces a result

> Tip: You can find the full event specification on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

 

 

Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have:

  • A BTP global account with administrative access
  • Your organization has completed onboarding to Unified Customer Landscape (UCL)
  • Entitlements for SAP Cloud Application Event Hub are available in your global account

> Note: If you haven't onboarded to UCL yet, this is a prerequisite for consuming business events. Please complete the UCL onboarding first. Refer to the SAP Cloud Application Event Hub documentation for details.

> Important: When onboarding to UCL, ensure that SPII notifications are not enabled. APM does not support SPII notifications yet. If SPII notifications are enabled during UCL onboarding, the integration will not work correctly.

 

 

Overview of the Setup Flow

Before you begin, decide which consumer will receive the events. The rest of the flow is largely the same either way:

  • SAP Integration Suite (recommended) — appears automatically in your System Landscape once entitled. No manual service instance or system registration is needed. Suite users skip Steps 4 and 5.
  • Your own BTP application — requires creating an Event Hub service instance (Step 4) and registering the app in the System Landscape (Step 5).

The overall process has five main stages:

1.  BTP Setup — Assign entitlements, subscribe to the Event Hub application, and assign the admin role (Steps 1–3, required for both consumer types)
2.  Custom Consumer Application Setup (custom BTP application only) — Create the Event Hub service instance and register your app in the System Landscape (Steps 4–5). Integration Suite users skip this stage
3.  UCL Formation — Connect APM, Event Hub, and your consumer (Integration Suite or custom app) in a Formation of type Eventing Between SAP Cloud Systems (Step 6)
4. Integration Dependencies & Subscriptions — Define which event types your consumer receives and activate them in the Event Hub UI (Steps 7–8)
5. APM Rule Configuration — Enable External Events as an output on your APM rules and verify execution (Step 9)

 

 

Step 1: Configure Global Account Entitlements

First, assign the required entitlements in your BTP global account.

1. Open the SAP BTP Cockpit and navigate to your global account.
2. Go to Entitlements > Entity Assignments and search for SAP Cloud Application Event Hub.
3. Add the following plans and assign quota:

Service / Application Service Technical Name Plan Purpose

SAP Cloud Application Event Hubevent-brokerevent-connectivityConnects your BTP consumer application
SAP Cloud Application Event Hubevent-mesh-sap2sapstandard (Application)The Event Hub UI

> Note: Service Technical Name is the internal identifier shown in the BTP cockpit's technical view (and used by CLI/API calls). Use it to disambiguate between plans if the display name is the same.

> Note: The event-mesh-multi-tenant plan is used automatically when APM is added to the UCL Formation. The event-broker / event-connectivity plan is used for the service instance you create for your custom consumer application (Step 4). SAP Integration Suite users do not need this plan.

> Region availability: Before creating your subaccount, check the availability tooltip on the entitlements page to confirm that Event Hub is available in your desired region. The screenshot below shows the currently supported regions.

 

 

Step 2: Create or Configure Your Subaccount

1. In your global account, click Create > Subaccount.
2. Provide a name and select the region (e.g., cf-eu20). Make sure this matches your APM deployment region — publisher and consumer must be in the same region.
3. Click Create.
4. Navigate into the new subaccount and go to Entitlements. Add the same plans you configured at global account level.

> Important: Publisher (APM) and consumer applications must be in the same region. Mismatched regions will cause the Formation validation to fail.

Configure Trust (Identity Provider)

For the Event Hub application UI and your consumer application to work correctly, your subaccount must have a custom Identity Authentication Service (IAS) tenant configured as a trusted identity provider.

1. In your subaccount, go to Security > Trust Configuration.
2. Verify that a Custom Identity Provider for Applications entry is present and Active — pointing to your IAS tenant (e.g., <tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com).

> Note: If this trust is missing, click Establish Trust and follow the wizard to connect your IAS tenant. This trust is required for the Event Hub administrator role (assigned in Step 3) to work with business users.

 

 

Step 3: Subscribe to the Event Hub Application and Assign Roles

This step enables the SAP Cloud Application Event Hub application in your subaccount, which provides the UI where you can manage subscriptions.

1. In your subaccount, go to Service Marketplace.
2. Search for SAP Cloud Application Event Hub and select the event-mesh-sap2sap / standard (Application) plan.
3. Click Create and complete the subscription.

After the subscription is created, assign the administration role:

4. Go to Security > Role Collections.
5. Find the role collection Event Broker Integration Administrator.
6. Assign this role collection to your user.

> Note: The role collection retains the former product name "Event Broker Integration Administrator". This role allows you to manage subscriptions in the Event Hub application UI, including enabling and disabling event subscriptions.

 

 

Steps 4 & 5: Custom Consumer Application Setup

> Note: Skip Steps 4 and 5 if using SAP Integration Suite. Steps 4 and 5 are only required if you want to develop your own BTP application to receive SAP Event Hub events. The recommended approach is to use SAP Integration Suite, which will appear automatically in your System Landscape. Suite users can proceed directly to Step 6.

The next two steps configure a custom consumer application:

  • Step 4 — create the Event Hub and Cloud Identity service instances (with the required bindings)
  • Step 5 — register the consumer application in the BTP System Landscape so the UCL Formation in Step 6 can discover it

 

 

Step 4: Create the Event Hub and Cloud Identity Service Instances

You can create the required service instances, trust, and bindings in one of two ways:

  • Automatically — deploy the sample CAP Event Broker MTA and let it provision everything (Step 4.1)
  • Manually — create each instance and binding yourself in the BTP cockpit (Step 4.2)

 

Step 4.1: Create the Required Instances and Trust Bindings Automatically via Your Event Broker Deployment

GitHub Repository (SAP-internal): Example CAP Event Broker

> Note: The repository above is hosted on SAP's internal GitHub instance and is not accessible to external customers. If you don't have access, follow the manual setup in Step 4.2 or use the public CAP Messaging — Event Broker guide as a starting point.

Prerequisites:

  • Node.js (>= v20)
  • npm (>= v10)
  • Cloud Foundry CLI (for deployment)

Installations:

  • npm add @cap-js/event-broker

The mta.yaml file contains specifications for the required service instances, trust, and bindings. When the custom app is deployed, they are all created automatically.

Optional: The app-identifier, systemNamespace, and srv-url can be modified in the mta.yaml file. Locate and adjust the following properties:

srv-url: ${default-url}       # webhook URL for your event consumer service instance
app-identifier: apm.broker    # any value, e.g., reuse the MTA ID
systemNamespace: apm.broker

> Note: The systemNamespace must not start with "sap", because this custom CAP event broker is not a default SAP application (see: System Namespace Naming Conventions). It must also be unique within your global account.

> Note: The systemNamespace must not start with "sap", because this custom CAP event broker is not a default SAP application (see: System Namespace Naming Conventions). It must also be unique within your global account.

 

Step 4.2: Create the Required Instances and Trust Bindings Manually

After you have built your CAP event broker, you must establish its connectivity to the SAP Cloud Application Event Hub application as well as its trust settings.

4.2.1 Create the Connectivity Service Instance

This service instance represents your consumer application's connection to Event Hub.

1. In your subaccount, go to Instances and Subscriptions.
2. Click Create.
3. Configure as follows:

Field Value

ServiceSAP Cloud Application Event Hub
Planevent-connectivity
Instance NameAny meaningful name (e.g., event-hub-consumer)

4. Provide the following JSON parameters, replacing the placeholders with your values:

{
  "namespace": "<your-customer-namespace>",
  "webhookUrl": "https://<your-consumer-endpoint>/webhook"
}

5. Click Create.

Parameter Description

namespaceYour customer-specific namespace for receiving events.
webhookUrlThe publicly accessible endpoint of your consumer application. Event Hub will POST events to this URL whenever a subscribed APM event fires.

> Note: The webhookUrl must be reachable from the internet. If you are using a CAP application deployed on BTP, this is the URL of your CAP service's event handler endpoint. Refer to the CAP Messaging documentation for native Event Hub support.

 

4.2.2 Create a Cloud Identity Services Instance

The authentication between Event Hub and your applications uses X.509 certificates via SAP Cloud Identity Services.

1. In your subaccount, go to Instances and Subscriptions.
2. Click Create.
3. Configure as follows:

Field Value

ServiceCloud Identity Services
Planapplication
Instance NameAny name of your choice

4. Provide the following JSON parameters:

{
  "consumed-services": [
    {
      "service-instance-name": "<your-event-hub-instance-name>"
    }
  ]
}

5. Click Create.

> Note: The consumed-services array links this identity service instance to your Event Hub instance, enabling secure communication between them.

 

4.2.3 Create Service Bindings

Now you create bindings that provide credentials for secure communication.

First, you must bind to your Cloud Identity Services instance.

1. Navigate to your Cloud Identity Services instance.
2. Click Create Binding (or Create Service Key).
3. Provide the following JSON parameters:

{
  "credential-type": "X509_GENERATED",
  "key-length": 2048,
  "validity": 100,
  "validity-type": "DAYS"
}

4. Click Create.

Second, you must bind to the Event Hub service instance.

1. Navigate to your Event Hub service instance.
2. Click Create Binding (or Create Service Key).
3. Provide the following JSON parameters:

{
  "authentication-type": "X509_IAS"
}

4. Click Create.

> Note: The X509_IAS authentication type tells Event Hub to use X.509 certificates from Cloud Identity Services for mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication. The generated certificate has a validity of 100 days — remember to rotate it before expiry.

 

 

 

Step 5: Register Your Consumer Application in the System Landscape

The UCL Formation (Step 6) requires every participant — including your consumer application — to be registered as a system in the BTP System Landscape.

1. In your global account, navigate to System Landscape > Systems.
2. Click Add System.
3. Fill in the details:

Field Value

System TypeSAP BTP Application
System NameA meaningful name (e.g., event-consumer-app)
System IDA UUID that identifies this application instance
System NamespaceA unique namespace for your app (e.g., apm.broker)

4. Click Add.

> Note: The System Namespace must be unique within your global account. It is used internally by the UCL Formation to match this system registration with the Event Hub service instance created in Step 4.

 

 

Step 6: Set Up the UCL Formation

The Unified Customer Landscape (UCL) Formation ties the three participants together: APM (publisher), SAP Cloud Application Event Hub (router), and your consumer system (subscriber) — either SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application.

6.1 Create the Formation

1. In your global account, navigate to System Landscape > Formations.
2. Click Create Formation.
3. Fill in the General Information:

Field Value

Formation Namee.g., Event Hub for SAP APM
Formation TypeEventing Between SAP Cloud Systems

4. Click Next Step.

6.2 Add Systems to the Formation

5. In the Include Systems step, you will see systems filtered by type. Select your consumer system — either SAP Integration Suite or your custom application (e.g., event-consumer-app).

6. Click Next Step / Create to finalize.

6.3 Verify the Formation is Ready

After creation, the Formation will appear in Draft status while the platform validates that every system can be matched to a corresponding service instance. Once validation succeeds, the status changes to Ready.

> What the Formation contains:

> - Your APM system (System Type: SAP Asset Performance Management) — discovered automatically from the subaccount

> - SAP Cloud Application Event Hub — created automatically after the subscription in Step 3

> - Your consumer system — either SAP Integration Suite (discovered automatically once entitled) or a custom SAP BTP Application registered manually in Step 5

> Important: If the Formation stays in Draft or validation fails:

> - For a custom BTP application: check that the System Namespace of your consumer matches the namespace parameter of your Event Hub service instance (Step 4), and that service instance parameters (especially ceSource and deploymentRegion) match the system registration.

> - For SAP Integration Suite: confirm the Suite subaccount is in the same region as APM and Event Hub, and that the Suite tenant is visible in the System Landscape.

 

 

Step 7: Create Integration Dependencies

Integration Dependencies define exactly which event types your consumer subscribes to from the APM publisher. Create them on your consumer system — either SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application. You need to create one Integration Dependency per APM rule type (Monitoring, Derivation, Aggregation).

1. In System Landscape > Systems, open your consumer system (SAP Integration Suite, or your custom app such as event-consumer-app).
2. Navigate to the Integration Dependencies tab.
3. Click Add.
4. Select the "Simplified Business Eventing" template and click Next Step.
6. Fill in the Dependency Details:

Field Value (example for Monitoring)

NameIntegration Dependency to Publish External Events as Rule Outputs (Monitoring)
Description<Your description>
Version1.0.0
Publishing System Namespacesap.apm
Event Typessap.apm.MonitoringRule.Run.v1

7. Click Review and then Add.

7. Repeat for the remaining two rule types, using the corresponding event types:

  • Derivation: sap.apm.DerivationRule.Run.v1
  • Aggregation: sap.apm.AggregationRule.Run.v1

After creating all three, the Integration Dependencies tab of your consumer system should show three entries:

 

> Note: Each Integration Dependency represents one subscription channel. Creating them separately per rule type keeps the subscriptions independent — you can enable, disable, or update them individually without affecting the others.

> Note: If you want to create one integration dependency for all event types, enter all three event types in the event types field separated by commas.

> Important: To update an Integration Dependency later (e.g., change the event type), you must create a new version. This requires briefly disabling the old subscription and enabling the new one, which causes a short interruption in event delivery.

 

 

Step 8: Enable Subscriptions in the Event Hub Application

Now you activate the Integration Dependencies you created in Step 7, turning them into live event subscriptions.

1. Open the SAP Cloud Application Event Hub application. Find the link in your subaccount under Instances and Subscriptions > Subscriptions.
2. Navigate to the "All Available Subscriptions" tab.
3. You should see the three Integration Dependencies you created in Step 7, all with status + Ready (meaning they are available to be activated but not yet enabled).

4. Click the > arrow on one subscription to open its detail panel. Verify that:

  • The Publisher is your APM system
  • The Subscriber is your consumer system (SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application)
  • The correct Event Type is shown (e.g., sap.apm.AggregationRule.Run.v1)

5. Toggle the subscription switch to enabled. A confirmation dialog appears — click Enable.

6. After enabling, the subscription status changes to Active.

7. Repeat for the remaining two subscriptions.

> Note: The "My Subscriptions" tab shows all currently active subscriptions. You can disable a subscription at any time from this view.

> What happens in the background: Once a subscription is enabled, Event Hub creates the eventing flow between the APM publisher and your consumer's webhook URL. From this point on, any APM rule event that fires will be routed to your consumer — SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application.

 

 

Step 9: Enable External Events as Rule Output in APM

With the infrastructure in place, the last configuration step is to enable External Events as an output on your APM rules.

1. Open SAP Asset Performance Management and navigate to Manage Rules.
2. Open an existing rule or create a new one.

Example: Create a Monitoring Rule Resulting in External Events

In this example, we use a Monitoring Rule called Minute_Based_Barometer_Reading.

1. On the Input and Output tab, find the Output section and click Edit.
2. Check the External Events checkbox (in addition to, or instead of, Alert/Notification).
3. Save the rule.

4. Go to the Settings tab to configure the rule trigger and the External Events section. The Event Group field here maps to the event payload's grouping key — set it to a meaningful value (e.g., barometerReading).

5. On the Condition tab, define the rule logic. For testing purposes a condition of true ensures the rule always fires and generates an event on every execution.

6. Click Activate to activate the rule. The rule status changes from Inactive to Active and a Next Scheduled On timestamp appears.

Once the rule is active and has executed at least once, you can confirm that External Events are being published successfully by checking the APM rule logs.

Step 10: Verify Event Publication in APM Rule Logs

1. In APM, navigate to View Rule Logs.
2. Filter by your rule name if needed, then click Go.
3. In the Rule Execution Results column, you should see two green badges per execution: Alert and External Event.

The green External Event badge confirms that the event was successfully published to SAP Cloud Application Event Hub. Event Hub then routes it to your consumer — SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application's webhook endpoint.

> Tip: If you see the Alert badge but the External Event badge is missing or red, verify that:

> - The subscription in the Event Hub UI is Active (Step 8 )

> - The Formation is in Ready status (Step 6)

> - For a custom BTP application: the webhookUrl in your Event Hub service instance is reachable

> - For SAP Integration Suite: the relevant iFlow / receiver channel is deployed and reachable

 

 

Troubleshooting

If something isn't working end to end, use the checks below.

Formation stuck in Draft

  • APM publisher and consumer are in the same region (Step 2).
  • Subaccount trust configuration points to your custom IAS tenant and is Active (Step 2).
  • Custom BTP application: the System Namespace of your consumer (Step 5) matches the namespace parameter of your Event Hub service instance (Step 4), and your service instance parameters (especially ceSource and deploymentRegion) match the system registration.
  • SAP Integration Suite: the Suite tenant appears in System Landscape > Systems and is entitled in the same region as APM and Event Hub.

Subscription doesn't appear in the Event Hub UI

  • All three Integration Dependencies were created against the correct consumer system (Step 7) — either Integration Suite or your custom app.
  • The Publishing System Namespace is sap.apm (not your consumer's namespace).
  • The user opening the Event Hub UI has the Event Broker Integration Administrator role collection (Step 3).

APM rule executes but the External Event badge is missing or red

  • The relevant subscription in the Event Hub UI is Active (Step 8).
  • The Formation is in Ready status (Step 6).
  • Custom BTP application: the webhookUrl in your Event Hub service instance is reachable from the internet, and the Cloud Identity Services binding certificate has not expired (default validity is 100 days).
  • SAP Integration Suite: the iFlow / receiver channel is deployed and its endpoint is reachable.

Events published but not received by the consumer

  • The consumer's endpoint returns a 2xx response to Event Hub's POST.
  • Custom BTP application: mTLS is correctly configured on both sides (the X509_IAS binding is bound to your app).
  • SAP Integration Suite: the receiver iFlow logs show the incoming request; if it does, the issue is in your Suite mapping, not the pipeline.

 

 

Summary

You have successfully set up the complete event flow from SAP APM Rules based on embedded IoT through SAP Cloud Application Event Hub to your consumer — either SAP Integration Suite or your custom BTP application.

Component What was configured

Global AccountEntitlements for event-connectivity and standard (Application) plans
SubaccountRegion-matched deployment with trust configured for IAS
Event Hub ApplicationSubscription + Event Broker Integration Administrator role
Event Hub Service Instance(Custom app only) Consumer namespace and webhook URL
System LandscapeConsumer registered — Integration Suite auto-discovered, or custom app added as SAP BTP Application
UCL FormationFormation type "Eventing Between SAP Cloud Systems" connecting APM, Event Hub, and consumer
Integration DependenciesThree dependencies — one per APM rule type (Monitoring, Derivation, Aggregation)
Event Hub SubscriptionsAll three subscriptions enabled and Active
APM RulesExternal Events checked as output; rule activated and confirmed via rule logs

Architecture Overview

Next Steps

  • Build your event handler: Implement business logic in your consumer — a receiver iFlow in SAP Integration Suite or an event handler in your custom BTP application — to react to received events (e.g., create maintenance orders, send alerts, update dashboards).
  • Explore CAP integration: If you're building a custom app with CAP, the CAP Messaging documentation provides native Event Hub support with minimal boilerplate.
  • Scale to more rules: Repeat Step 9 for every APM rule you want to publish events. No additional infrastructure changes are needed — all rules of the same type share the same subscription channel.

Useful Links

  • SAP Cloud Application Event Hub Documentation
  • Rules based on embedded IoT Events API
  • Creating Event Connectivity Service Instance
  • CAP Messaging — Event Broker
  • Cloud Identity Services Reference

 

 

If you have questions or feedback, feel free to leave a comment below. Happy eventing!

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks

Part 3 of the SAP Event Mesh beginner series.

A quick recap

By the end of Part 2, you had events publishing into Event Mesh over HTTP and CPI consuming them from a queue.

Part 1 (this post):  EDA concepts + provisioning the Event Mesh instance, queues, subscriptions, and service key (https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-1-event-driven-architecture-topics-queues/ba-p/14428493)

Part 2: Testing with Postman + CPI iFlow configuration (publish & consume) (https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-2-testing-with-postman-amp-cpi-iflow/ba-p/14428855)

Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks 
Part 4:  Common errors, fixes, and a quick-reference cheat sheet

But there's a catch with everything we've done so far. Every consumption approach in Part 2 was pull-based — your CPI iFlow (AMQP Sender, or the REST /consumption call) reaches out to the queue and asks "any messages for me?" That works, but it means CPI is doing the polling, and there's always a small delay.

There's a second delivery model that flips this around: the webhook. Instead of CPI pulling, Event Mesh pushes — the moment a message lands in the queue, Event Mesh calls your endpoint. No polling, near real-time, and it's the pattern most S/4HANA → CPI integrations actually use in the wild.

If you went looking for "webhook" on the Queue screen and couldn't find it — you're not missing anything. It lives somewhere else, and we'll get there.

 

1. Pull vs Push — when to use which

Both models start the same way: a queue subscribed to your topics collects messages. The difference is who initiates delivery.

  Pull (Part 2) Push (this post)
Who initiatesCPI reaches out to the queueEvent Mesh calls your endpoint
CPI adapterAMQP Sender / REST consumptionHTTPS Sender (receives the call)
TimingPolling intervalNear real-time
Best forBatch-style / CPI-driven pacingEvent-driven, low-latency S/4HANA → CPI

A webhook subscription connects your webhook to a queue, and when messages reach that queue, it delivers them to your webhook in near real time. Because there's no polling, it consumes fewer resources on both sides.

For most "react to an S/4HANA business event" scenarios, push (webhook) is the right choice.

2. Build the receiving iFlow first

A webhook needs somewhere to push to — an HTTPS endpoint that's ready and waiting. In CPI, that's an iFlow with an HTTP Sender adapter. Its address becomes your webhook URL.

  1. Create a new iFlow (e.g. EventMesh_Webhook_Receiver).
  2. On the Sender → HTTPS adapter:
    • Address: something like /eventmesh-webhook → the full URL becomes https://<your-cpi-tenant>/http/eventmesh-webhook
    • Authorization: User Role, role ESBMessaging.send (this is what the webhook's OAuth token will need permission for).
  3. Add whatever processing you need after the sender (map the CloudEvent, call an OData API, etc.).
  4. Deploy the iFlow — the endpoint must be live before you create the webhook, or the handshake (next section) will fail.

 

✅Tip: Copy the deployed endpoint URL from Monitor → Manage Integration Content → your iFlow → Endpoints. That exact URL is what you'll paste as the webhook URL.

3. Get the credential that lets the webhook call into CPI

Here's the single most important thing to understand about webhooks, and where almost everyone gets confused:

⚠️The webhook authenticates INTO CPI — so it uses CPI's inbound credential, not the Event Mesh service key.

Everything up to now used the Event Mesh key (so CPI could talk to Event Mesh). A webhook is the opposite direction: Event Mesh is now the caller, and it needs credentials to get into your CPI endpoint.

Those credentials come from a Process Integration Runtime service instance:

  1. In your CPI subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → Create → service Process Integration Runtime, plan integration-flow.
  2. Add the role/grant for ESBMessaging.send (API access).
  3. Create a Service Key on it → note the clientid, clientsecret, and tokenurl.

These three values are what secure the webhook. Keep them separate in your head from the Event Mesh key — mixing them up is the #1 webhook failure.

4. Create the webhook

Now the part you couldn't find. It's not on the Queue screen — it's on the message client.

  1. In Event Mesh, open the Overview tab and click your Message Client → this opens the Webhooksconfiguration screen → click Create.
  2. Fill in:
Field Value
Namewebhook-3pl (any name)
Queue NameS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3pl (the queue to attach to)
Webhook URLhttps://<your-cpi-tenant>/http/eventmesh-webhook (the deployed HTTPS Sender endpoint)
Quality of Service1 (recommended — see section 6)
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Client ID / Secret / Token URLfrom the Process Integration Runtime service key (section 3)
  1. Save. On success you'll see the subscription added.

 

From here, any message that lands in the 3pl queue is pushed to your iFlow — no polling, no AMQP Sender needed.

5. The handshake (what it is, why it fails)

Before Event Mesh sends a single real message, it does a handshake to make sure your endpoint actually agreed to receive pushes.

The handshake is an OPTIONS call to your webhook URL carrying a WebHook-Request-Origin header. Your endpoint must reply with a WebHook-Allowed-Origin header whose value matches that origin (or is *). Only after a successful handshake does Event Mesh start publishing messages to the webhook.

The good news: a properly deployed CPI HTTPS Sender endpoint handles this automatically. The usual reasons a handshake fails:

  • The iFlow isn't deployed or the endpoint isn't reachable.
  • The credentials are wrong (remember: CPI runtime key, not Event Mesh key).
  • The endpoint requires an auth the webhook isn't sending.

If the webhook shows as not active or "handshake failed," check those three before anything else.

6. QoS and the retry behavior

When you create the webhook you pick a Quality of Service:

  • QoS 0 (at most once): Event Mesh posts the message to your webhook and deletes it from the queue regardlessof whether your endpoint succeeded. Fast, but you can lose messages if CPI was down.
  • QoS 1 (at least once): Event Mesh posts the message and waits for a 2XX response as acknowledgment. Any 2XX = success, message removed. Anything else = the message stays and gets retried.

That retry behavior is worth knowing: on a non-2XX response the service resends roughly every 15 minutes, gradually slowing the frequency, and keeps redelivering for up to 30 days unless your webhook positively acknowledges. So a broken downstream won't silently drop events — but it also means a misbehaving endpoint can pile up retries.

✅Tip: For anything you can't afford to lose (master data, business events), use QoS 1. You can pause a webhook during maintenance and resume it later without losing queued messages.

7. Troubleshooting

Symptom Likely cause Fix
Handshake failed / webhook not activeiFlow not deployed, or endpoint unreachableDeploy the iFlow; confirm the endpoint URL from Monitor → Endpoints
401 on deliveryWrong credential direction — used the Event Mesh keyUse the Process Integration Runtime client id/secret/token URL
403 on deliveryOAuth client lacks the API roleGrant ESBMessaging.send to the CPI runtime instance
Messages pile up in the queue, never deliveredEndpoint returns non-2XX (QoS 1 keeps retrying)Fix the receiving iFlow so it returns 2XX; check CPI monitoring
Nothing arrives at allQueue isn't subscribed to the right topic (case-sensitive!)Re-check the queue's topic subscription — same gotcha as the rest of the series

8. Quick Reference

🔒Replace all placeholders with your own values. Never publish real secrets.

Item Value
Where to createEvent Mesh → Overview → Message Client → Webhooks → Create
Webhook URLhttps://<your-cpi-tenant>/http/<iflow-address> (deployed HTTPS Sender endpoint)
Auth typeOAuth2 Client Credentials
Auth sourceProcess Integration Runtime service key (NOT the Event Mesh key)
Required CPI roleESBMessaging.send
HandshakeOPTIONS + WebHook-Request-Origin → reply WebHook-Allowed-Origin (match or *)
QoS 0delete after post, regardless of result
QoS 1needs 2XX ack; retries ~15 min, backs off, up to 30 days
Maintenancepause / resume the webhook

What's next

Webhooks complete the delivery picture: Part 2 gave you the pull model (CPI reaches out), and this post gives you the push model (Event Mesh reaches in). For real-time S/4HANA → CPI event handling, push is usually what you want.

The one idea to carry away: credential direction. Publishing/consuming uses the Event Mesh key; the webhook uses your CPI runtime key, because the caller and callee have swapped.

In Part 4 — Troubleshooting & Quick Reference, we'll bring the whole series together: every error you're likely to hit across setup, publishing, consuming, and webhooks — with causes, fixes, and a one-page cheat sheet to keep open while you build.

🔒Security note: All client IDs, secrets, tenant URLs, and endpoints in this post are placeholders. Use your own values, keep them out of public posts, and rotate anything that's been exposed.

Primary Tag: SAP Integration Suite Tags: SAP Event Mesh, Cloud Integration, SAP CPI, Webhooks, OAuth2, Event-Driven Architecture, SAP BTP
